claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand a bit about your situation.

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

1. **What is the name and age of the child** you want to claim Child Benefit for?
   - If you have multiple children, please tell me about each of them

The eligibility criteria differ based on the child's age:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Young people **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please share the details about your child or children, and we'll work through the eligibility criteria together.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to claim for - Alex, who's 10 years old, and Blake, who's just 2. They both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you're looking into claiming Child Benefit for Alex (10 years old) and Blake (2 years old).

Since both children are **under 16**, they're in the age category that generally qualifies automatically. Now let me ask you a few questions to confirm their eligibility.

**First, let me check about their work status:**

Are either Alex or Blake working full-time (meaning 24 hours or more per week)?

This is a standard question we need to ask, though it's quite rare for children of their ages to be working.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are working at all. Alex is in school and Blake is just a toddler, so no work for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working full-time, let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits

Again, this is very unlikely given their ages, but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just kids, so they don't have anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's what I expected! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are either Alex or Blake currently enrolled in an apprenticeship program?

(Again, given their ages - 10 and 2 - this is just a formality, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. They're way too young for that - Alex is still in primary school and Blake is just a toddler!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes perfect sense! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about **your residency status** as the claimant.

**Do any of the following apply to you?**

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)

2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme

3. You have pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme AND meet one of these conditions:
   - Currently working and earning above the National Insurance threshold
   - Actively looking for work (up to 91 days)
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself and your family
   - Are studying with sufficient resources
   - Are a family member of an EEA or Swiss national with right to reside

4. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to your situation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK at the moment. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Thank you for letting me know that you're currently living abroad. This is an important factor for Child Benefit eligibility.

**Let me ask some follow-up questions to determine if you might still qualify:**

1. **Are you working abroad as a Crown servant** (e.g., civil servant, diplomat, member of HM Forces)?

2. **Or are you living in one of the following qualifying countries?**
   - A European Economic Area (EEA) country
   - Switzerland
   - Gibraltar
   - A country covered by the UK's reciprocal agreement

3. **Or do you have any connection to the UK**, such as:
   - Do you still pay UK National Insurance contributions?
   - Are you on a temporary posting abroad?
   - Do you plan to return to live in the UK?

Please tell me more about your situation abroad - where you're living and why you're there. This will help me determine if you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad with my family - not in any of those special categories you mentioned. We're not temporarily posted or paying UK National Insurance either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying your situation. I appreciate your honesty.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Decision**

I'm sorry to inform you that based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit** for Alex or Blake at this time.

### **Why You're Not Eligible:**

While both **Alex (age 10) and Blake (age 2)** meet all the child-specific requirements for Child Benefit, the issue is with the **residency requirements for claimants**.

To claim Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or meet one of the special circumstances, such as:
- Being a Crown servant working abroad
- Living in a qualifying country with a reciprocal agreement (EEA countries, Switzerland, Gibraltar, etc.)
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme with qualifying conditions
- Paying UK National Insurance contributions while temporarily abroad

Since you're living abroad and don't fall into any of these special categories, you unfortunately don't meet the fundamental residency requirement for claiming Child Benefit.

### **What You Can Do:**

- **If you return to live in the UK** in the future, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that time for any qualifying children
- **Check the benefits system** in the country where you currently live - they may have similar family benefit programs available
- If your circumstances change (e.g., you start paying UK National Insurance contributions or move to a qualifying country), you may become eligible

I understand this isn't the outcome you were hoping for, but please don't hesitate to reach out again if your situation changes or if you have any other questions.

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old and meets the age requirements (under 16), is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements necessary to claim Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad, is not a Crown servant, does not live in a qualifying country with reciprocal agreements, and does not pay UK National Insurance contributions. Without meeting the residency requirements, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake is 2 years old and meets the age requirements (under 16), is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements necessary to claim Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad, is not a Crown servant, does not live in a qualifying country with reciprocal agreements, and does not pay UK National Insurance contributions. Without meeting the residency requirements, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children (Alex, age 10, and Blake, age 2) who both meet the child-specific eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements, which is a fundamental requirement for claiming Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad and does not fall into any of the special categories that would allow them to claim while outside the UK (such as Crown servants, those in qualifying countries with reciprocal agreements, or those with settled/pre-settled status meeting specific conditions). Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
